title: Why Corporate America Fails Working Mothers (And How to Fix It)
guestBio: Cassie Leonard is an engineer, two-time bestselling author, and founder of ELMM Coaching. She spent 16 years climbing the corporate ladder in aerospace engineering before recognizing the system wasn't built for working mothers and pivoted to help working parents in STEMM design careers that actually work.
excerpt: Working mothers aren't failing—they're operating inside a system never designed for them. Cassie Leonard breaks down what the corporate world gets wrong about women, motherhood, and leadership, plus how to engineer a career that doesn't break you.
metaDescription: Why the 9-to-5 fails working mothers + how to redesign your career. Cassie Leonard shares insights on invisible labor, matrescence & leadership.
keyTakeaways: The 9-to-5 workday was designed around a worker who has a wife at home handling everything else, not modern dual-career families.
Working mothers must consciously refuse to absorb invisible labor at work to avoid being pigeonholed into support roles.
Equality doesn't mean treating everyone the same—it means accounting for the whole person and their circumstances.
Matrescence is the biological brain restructuring that happens during motherhood, and the postpartum struggle has a scientific name.
Partnership at home requires explicit conversations about mental load and task ownership that society tells us not to have.
Community must be intentionally engineered rather than waiting for it to naturally appear.
Motherhood teaches more leadership skills than any corporate training program but remains undervalued in professional settings.

pullQuotes.text: Working mothers aren't behind—they're operating inside a system never designed for them

quiz.title: What's Your Working Mother Leadership Style?
quiz.description: Discover how you naturally navigate the intersection of motherhood and career, and learn why this episode will help you thrive.

A.name: System Engineer
A.description: You see the big picture and naturally identify what's broken in the systems around you. You're strategic about where you spend your energy and aren't afraid to challenge outdated structures. Your superpower is creating efficient processes that work for real life, not just corporate ideals. This episode will validate your instincts about why traditional work structures feel so impossible and give you language to articulate the changes you know need to happen.

B.name: Community Builder
B.description: You understand that success isn't a solo journey and you're willing to invest in relationships that support everyone's growth. You naturally create connections and see the value in lifting others as you climb. Your strength lies in recognizing that we're all better when we work together. This episode will give you permission to be even more intentional about building the professional and personal support systems you know are essential for working mothers.

C.name: Boundary Architect
C.description: You've learned through experience that saying yes to everything means saying no to what matters most. You're thoughtful about protecting your time and energy, even when others don't understand your choices. Your wisdom comes from knowing that sustainable success requires clear limits. This episode will reinforce your approach and help you articulate why boundaries aren't selfish—they're strategic for long-term success as a working mother.

D.name: Value Translator
D.description: You see the skills and strength that motherhood develops, even when others don't recognize them. You're passionate about changing perceptions and helping the world see working mothers as assets, not liabilities. Your gift is connecting seemingly different experiences to show their true worth. This episode will give you powerful new language and frameworks to advocate for yourself and other working mothers in professional settings.

questions.question: When you're in a work meeting that's running long and you need to pick up your kids, you typically:

options.text: Leave at your planned time without extensive explanation—your calendar boundaries exist for a reason
Stay late and text your partner or another parent to handle pickup, then feel guilty about it later
Speak up about the meeting running over and suggest ways to wrap up more efficiently
Use it as a teachable moment about how parents need predictable schedules to be their best at work

questions.question: When colleagues automatically assume you'll organize the office celebration or take meeting notes, you:

options.text: Politely redirect by saying you're focused on other priorities and suggest rotating responsibilities
Take it on because you want to maintain good relationships, but make note of the pattern
Point out that these assumptions reflect outdated thinking about women's roles in the workplace
Decline and suggest a more systematic approach to distributing administrative tasks

questions.question: When other working parents in your company seem to be struggling, you're most likely to:

options.text: Reach out individually to offer support and see if you can help problem-solve together
Share resources and strategies that have worked for you while acknowledging everyone's situation is different
Advocate with leadership for policy changes that would support all working parents better
Start conversations about how parenting skills translate to valuable workplace competencies

questions.question: When you're feeling overwhelmed by competing demands from work and family, you typically:

options.text: Step back and analyze which systems or processes aren't working efficiently
Remind yourself that this feeling is normal and focus on what you can realistically accomplish today
Reflect on how these challenges are actually developing your leadership and problem-solving skills
Reach out to other working parents for perspective and mutual support

questions.question: When discussing your career goals, you tend to emphasize:

options.text: How you want to create more sustainable and effective ways of working
The importance of building workplaces where everyone can thrive
Achieving success while maintaining the personal values and priorities that matter to you
Changing perceptions about what working mothers bring to leadership roles

questions.question: When you see workplace policies that don't account for modern family realities, you're most likely to:

options.text: Document the gaps and propose specific alternative approaches
Work within existing constraints while protecting your own well-being
Connect with other employees who might be affected to build collective understanding
Frame the conversation around how inclusive policies drive better business outcomes

content:
If you're a working mother who feels like you're constantly running uphill while everyone else seems to be on flat ground, this episode is for you. The truth is, you're not failing—you're operating inside a system that was never designed for women like you.

Cassie Leonard knows this reality intimately. After spending 16 years climbing the corporate ladder in aerospace engineering, she had a revelation that changed everything: the 9-to-5 workday was built for a 1920s family structure where one person works outside the home while another handles everything else. That model doesn't work when both parents have careers, and it especially doesn't work for mothers navigating the biological and social realities of raising children.

The Invisible Labor That's Holding You Back

One of the most powerful insights Cassie shares is about invisible labor—both at home and at work. At work, this shows up as women automatically taking on the emotional labor, the coordination, the behind-the-scenes work that keeps teams functioning but doesn't get recognized or rewarded. Cassie made a conscious decision to refuse this invisible work, and it was game-changing for her career trajectory.

The key is recognizing that you have a choice in what work you absorb. When someone assumes you'll handle the team birthday parties, take notes in meetings, or manage interpersonal conflicts, you can redirect that energy toward work that actually advances your career. This isn't about being difficult—it's about being strategic with your time and energy.

Why Partnership Requires Uncomfortable Conversations

At home, Cassie emphasizes the difference between partnership and ownership. True partnership means having explicit conversations about who owns what tasks—not just who executes them, but who carries the mental load of remembering, planning, and coordinating. These conversations feel uncomfortable because society tells us that mothers should naturally know how to handle everything, but that's exactly why we need to have them.

Cassie shares a powerful story about a baby shower where she realized how society assumes mothers will automatically have everything under control. This assumption places an unfair burden on women and lets partners off the hook from truly sharing the cognitive load of running a household and raising children.

Understanding Matrescence: The Brain Change No One Warns You About

Here's something most women don't know: there's a scientific term for what happens to your brain when you become a mother. Matrescence is the biological restructuring that occurs during the transition to motherhood, similar to adolescence. Your brain literally changes to help you bond with and protect your child, but this process can also create feelings of identity loss and overwhelm.

Knowing that matrescence is real and biological—not a personal failing—can be incredibly validating for mothers struggling with the postpartum period. You're not weak or broken; you're experiencing a documented neurological transition that deserves support and understanding.

Engineering Your Community

One of Cassie's most actionable pieces of advice is about intentionally building community. Modern women often wait for community to happen naturally, but Cassie advocates for engineering your neighborhood—actively creating the support systems you need. This might mean organizing regular playdates, starting a working mothers' group in your area, or simply being the person who reaches out to build connections.

The isolation many working mothers feel isn't a personal failing; it's a structural problem that requires intentional solutions. You have permission to be proactive about building the village you need.

Redefining Productivity and Success

Cassie challenges the traditional corporate obsession with time-on-station over actual productivity. The idea that being present for more hours equals better performance is outdated and particularly harmful to working parents who need flexibility to manage their complex lives. Real productivity is about outcomes, not hours logged.

She also addresses the expiration date on hustle culture and the 'lean in' mentality. While these approaches may have served a purpose, they're no longer sustainable or effective for the modern working woman who's managing multiple priorities and responsibilities.

Motherhood as Leadership Training

Perhaps most importantly, Cassie reframes motherhood as the most undervalued leadership training in corporate America. Think about what you do as a mother: you negotiate, mediate conflicts, manage multiple projects simultaneously, adapt to changing circumstances, and make decisions with incomplete information—all while maintaining relationships and keeping everyone motivated. These are exactly the skills companies pay millions to develop in their executives.

Yet corporate America continues to see motherhood as a liability rather than an asset. This perception needs to change, and it starts with mothers themselves recognizing and articulating the value they bring to the workplace because of their parenting experience, not despite it.

If you're a working mother feeling stretched thin, remember that the system isn't designed for you—but that doesn't mean you can't succeed within it or work to change it. You have permission to refuse invisible labor, demand true partnership at home, build the community you need, and recognize your motherhood as the leadership experience it truly is.
